one_line_takeaway: A neuroscience-inspired hybrid Pavlovian/Instrumental-MF/Model-Based RL architecture with Bayesian MB/MF arbitration accelerates convergence and reduces excursions into hazardous (GPS-denied) regions for multi-agent RF-based target localization, in grid-world simulation only -- this is a robotics/multi-agent RL paper with no security content.
key_finding: Qualitative trajectory analysis: at Episode 1 agents show exploratory, loop-trapped, GPS-denied-entering behavior; by Episode 100 Pavlovian-modulated agents already show consistent gate-crossing and GPS-denied avoidance not yet present in purely instrumental agents; by Episode 1200 the full hybrid agents complete the localization task in 31 steps with efficient, collision-free, GPS-denied-avoiding trajectories. Comparing arbitration dynamics (Fig. 6): in the Instrumental MF-MB baseline the arbitration probability P_MB rises and stabilizes at a high value (MB-dominated) because the MF learner remains unreliable (frequently explores high-risk regions, producing large RPEs); in the proposed PIT MF-MB (Hybrid), Pavlovian modulation stabilizes the MF system's transitions early, so P_MB stabilizes at a comparatively lower/more balanced value rather than becoming MB-dominated. In head-to-head trajectory comparison (Fig. 7), the Instrumental MF-MB baseline grazes the GPS-denied region before eventually finding the target, whereas the PIT MF-MB (Hybrid) executes a decisive avoidance maneuver before reaching the hazardous zone. In the steps-to-goal comparison over 40 Monte Carlo runs (Fig. 8), the PIT MF-MB (Hybrid) reaches a competent (stabilized) policy earliest among all four variants, achieving the fastest stabilization at around 200 episodes; MB variants generally stabilize faster than pure MF variants; and adding the motivational signal M consistently reduces the number of steps needed to reach the PEB goal across all four architectures, with the effect most pronounced for Instrumental MF and PIT MF configurations (the paper does not report exact numeric step-count values for each curve beyond these qualitative/ordinal comparisons and the '~200 episodes' stabilization point).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a human-inspired hybrid reinforcement-learning (RL) architecture for autonomous multi-agent navigation and target localization. It combines three learning systems: (1) a Pavlovian module that learns state-value predictions from 'contextual radio cues' (gates = LOS transition zones, and GPS-denied regions) and converts them into action-dependent approach/avoidance biases via a modulation function ga(V); (2) an Instrumental Model-Free (MF) Q-learner for habitual action-value estimation; and (3) an Instrumental Model-Based (MB) planner using Dyna-Q for goal-directed rollouts. A Bayesian arbitration mechanism (Dirichlet reliability model over State Prediction Error/SPE and Reward Prediction Error/RPE) dynamically blends MF and MB Q-values (P_MB) into a hybrid Q-value used in a softmax policy also modulated by the Pavlovian bias term. A separate 'motivational gate' injects an additive cost term into TD updates based on normalized battery level and elapsed mission time, biasing learning toward resource-efficient trajectories. The framework is explicitly framed as translating Pavlovian-Instrumental Transfer (PIT) concepts from behavioral neuroscience into a computational RL control architecture.
threat_model: N/A. This is not a security paper. There is no adversary, attacker, threat model, vulnerability, or defense mechanism anywhere in the text. The paper's notion of 'risk' refers to physical/operational hazards for a robot swarm (collisions with walls/agents/target, entering GPS-denied regions that degrade localization accuracy), not adversarial or security risk.
limitations: The paper's own text and stated future work indicate: (1) the architecture uses tabular Q-tables/discrete grid-world state-action representations, not deep RL / continuous state-action spaces -- explicitly deferred to future work; (2) agents are independent learners with no explicit inter-agent communication of value functions or policies (coordination is implicit via the shared environment) -- multi-agent cooperation via shared Pavlovian/model signals is deferred to future work; (3) validation is purely in simulation; physical-platform validation under real-world sensing/environmental variability is explicitly deferred to future work; (4) the paper assumes the CS-outcome (cue-outcome) association between contextual radio cues (gates, GPS-denied regions) and task outcomes has already been established/validated by a separate prior algorithm (cited as their own related work, ref [36]), i.e., the cue-learning/discovery process itself is not solved here, only its downstream use; (5) transitions in the internal MB model are assumed deterministic in the grid-world; (6) no comparison against learned/deep-RL baselines from prior literature (e.g., PAL, MaxPain, cited in Related Work) is performed -- the comparative evaluation is only among the paper's own four ablation variants, not against these cited external methods.

datasets_used: No external dataset; a custom-built discrete 24x36 (1 m cell) 2D grid-world simulation environment with walls/obstacles, designated 'gate' zones, and GPS-denied regions, with N=4 simulated agents and one static RF-beacon target, evaluated over 40 Monte Carlo simulation runs and 1200 training episodes (max 800 steps/episode).
quantitative_results: Fully trained hybrid agents complete the localization task in 31 steps by Episode 1200
Pavlovian reward for gate transition r_gate = +8; penalty for entering GPS-denied region r_GD = -8 (per agent, per step)
Collision penalty lambda_col = 1.2; terminal goal reward R_goal = 100
Learning rate schedule: initial 0.55, decay rate 0.9985, final 0.09; discount factor gamma = 0.98

Softmax temperature: initial 1.2, decay rate 0.996, final 0.03; K=2 Dyna-Q planning steps; PIT balance beta=1; motivational weights xi1=xi2=0.4; initial P_MB=0.5; motivational balancing weight phi=0.6

PIT MF-MB (Hybrid) achieves the fastest policy stabilization at approximately 200 episodes, faster than the other three variants (Instrumental MF, PIT MF, Instrumental MF-MB), per Fig. 8's steps-per-episode curves averaged over 40 Monte Carlo runs

RF link parameters used for RSSI/PEB computation: Tx power -10 dBm, antenna gains 2 dBi, bandwidth 1 MHz at 2.4 GHz carrier, receiver noise figure 10 dB, wall loss 25 dB, path-loss exponent eta=2 (LOS) / 3.5 (NLOS), GPS-error covariance diag(100,100) m^2
baselines_compared: Instrumental MF: standard Q-learning + softmax, no Pavlovian modulation, no MB planning
PIT MF: Q-learning + Pavlovian modulation via ga(V), no MB planning
Instrumental MF-MB: Dyna-Q hybrid of QMF and QMB via Bayesian arbitration, no Pavlovian modulation
PIT MF-MB (Hybrid): the paper's full proposed method (Pavlovian + Instrumental MF + Instrumental MB + Bayesian arbitration)
Each of the above four also tested with vs. without the motivational signal M_i,t (8 total configurations)
